skill.name: wp-plugin-development
skill.description: Guides WordPress plugin development with architecture, lifecycle hooks, Settings API usage, data storage, cron, security checks, debugging, and release packaging.
skill.summary: Build and review WordPress plugins with secure, maintainable project patterns.
skill.author: Automattic

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many high and medium patterns, but review found they are mostly Markdown code spans, WordPress API names, documentation URLs, and regular-expression parsing. The only confirmed operational risk is a local Node.js helper that scans PHP files under the current working directory. No malicious intent, credential access, prompt injection, or data exfiltration evidence was found.

low_findings.title: Local Filesystem Plugin Detection
low_findings.description: The helper script recursively scans the current working directory and reads PHP files to detect WordPress plugin headers. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, bounded by maxFiles and maxDepth, and does not send data over the network. It still requires user awareness because it reads local project files and prints detected metadata.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly uses fs.statSync, fs.readFileSync, and fs.readdirSync, then writes a local detection report to stdout. The behavior is clearly scoped to plugin discovery and shows no network exfiltration path.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported command-execution locations are Markdown inline code examples, WordPress function names, superglobal names, or JavaScript regular-expression code. No child process execution, shell invocation, Ruby backtick execution, or dynamic command construction was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms these lines are documentation snippets or safe JavaScript string and regex operations. There is no import or use of child_process and no shell metacharacter execution path.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Are Benign References
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL findings point to official WordPress developer documentation pages used as references. They are not runtime fetches and do not transmit user data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each URL appears in Markdown reference material, not executable code. No HTTP client code or automatic network access is present in these files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Scanner Noise
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts do not correspond to cryptographic code. The flagged locations contain headings, WordPress compatibility text, SQL placeholder guidance, uninstall guardrails, or regular-expression construction for plugin header parsing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed lines do not call MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or any cryptographic API. The alerts appear caused by substring matching in documentation and parser code.

content.value_statement: WordPress plugin work often fails around hooks, settings, storage, and security review. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused guidance for safer plugin changes.
actual_capabilities: Guides plugin structure with a main bootstrap file and hook registration patterns.
Explains activation, deactivation, uninstall, and migration guardrails.
Provides Settings API guidance for admin options and validation callbacks.
Covers WordPress security basics for nonces, capabilities, sanitization, escaping, and SQL safety.
Includes a local script that detects plugin headers in PHP files.
Offers debugging routes for activation, settings, and plugin loading issues.
limitations: Requires an existing WordPress plugin repository or clear target plugin path.
Does not replace WordPress core documentation or project-specific review.
Some workflows require WP-CLI, PHP tooling, or repository test commands.
The detector script reads local PHP files and reports plugin metadata only.

use_cases.title: Create a Plugin Foundation
use_cases.description: Set up a maintainable plugin structure with clear bootstrap, includes, and hook registration.

use_cases.title: Review Security-Sensitive Changes
use_cases.description: Check settings forms, SQL access, nonces, capabilities, sanitization, and escaping before release.

use_cases.title: Prepare Release Cleanup
use_cases.description: Validate activation, deactivation, uninstall, migrations, cron behavior, and packaging steps.

prompt_templates.title: Locate Plugin Entrypoints
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the WordPress plugin entrypoints in this repository. Identify the main plugin file, plugin metadata, and any obvious structure concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Add an Admin Setting
prompt_templates.prompt: Add a WordPress admin setting using the Settings API. Include validation, capability checks, nonce handling, and escaped output.

prompt_templates.title: Review Lifecycle Behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this plugin lifecycle code for activation, deactivation, uninstall, rewrite flushing, migrations, and multisite concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Plugin Security
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this plugin for nonce misuse, missing capability checks, unsafe SQL, unsanitized input, unescaped output, and unsafe cleanup.

output_examples.input: Review a plugin settings page before release.
output: A prioritized list of missing capability checks, nonce issues, and escaping gaps.
Suggested WordPress APIs for each fix.
A verification checklist for saving and displaying settings.

output_examples.input: Plan activation and uninstall behavior for a plugin update.
output: A lifecycle plan covering activation hooks, rewrite rule flushing, schema versioning, and cleanup boundaries.
Risks to test on single site and multisite installs.

best_practices: Identify the exact plugin main file before changing code.
Pair every nonce check with an appropriate capability check.
Run available PHP tests, linting, and WordPress-specific checks before release.
anti_patterns: Do not register activation hooks inside other hooks.
Do not trust entire request arrays or concatenate user input into SQL.
Do not make uninstall routines delete data without explicit guardrails.

faq.question: Does this skill create complete plugins from scratch?
faq.answer: It can guide plugin creation, but it works best with a repository, target path, and clear requirements.

faq.question: Does it support multisite plugins?
faq.answer: Yes. It asks for single site or multisite context because lifecycle and capability behavior can differ.

faq.question: Can it run WP-CLI commands?
faq.answer: It can recommend WP-CLI workflows when available. The user controls which commands run in the workspace.

faq.question: Does it cover WordPress security review?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers nonces, capabilities, sanitization, escaping, SQL safety, and safe uninstall behavior.

faq.question: What does the detector script do?
faq.answer: It scans local PHP files under the current directory and reports files that contain WordPress plugin headers.

faq.question: Is this skill tied to one plugin framework?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on WordPress core APIs and common plugin architecture patterns.
